Optimal average power for Bronze oxide removal without substrate damage
Near-IR wavelength with good absorption in Bronze oxides and moderate substrate absorption
Optimal spot diameter for balancing cleaning efficiency and processing speed
High repetition rate for efficient cleaning coverage while maintaining pulse energy
Fluence threshold for effective Bronze oxide removal while preserving substrate
Nanosecond pulse duration for efficient oxide layer ablation with minimal thermal diffusion
Optimal scanning speed for uniform oxide removal without substrate damage
Recommended number of passes for complete oxide removal with safety margin
Optimal pulse overlap for uniform cleaning coverage without excessive thermal buildup

Typical range for industrial 1064 nm fiber lasers cleaning metals like bronze; starting at 200W balances efficiency and safety to avoid overheating due to bronze's high reflectivity and thermal conductivity (~100-300 W/m·K). Max limited to prevent substrate melting (~950°C). Source: IPG Photonics YLR series specs and studies on copper alloys (e.g., J. Laser Appl. 2018).

Effective range for contaminant removal without excessive heat buildup; slower (500-1500 mm/s) for heavy oxidation, faster (2000-5000 mm/s) for light dirt. Recommended 2000 mm/s for optimal bronze cleaning to ensure uniform ablation of contaminants. Source: Industry standards from Laserax and Adapt Laser Systems manuals.

Nanosecond regime preferred for cost-effective cleaning of reflective metals; 5-20 ns minimizes heat-affected zone (HAZ <10 μm) compared to longer pulses, while picosecond (ps) or femtosecond (fs) reduce thermal damage further but increase cost. 20 ns typical for 1064 nm Nd:YAG on bronze. Source: Laser physics research (Opt. Lasers Eng. 2015; ablation studies on Cu-Sn alloys).

Standard for pulsed cleaning to achieve high throughput; 10-50 kHz for bronze to balance speed and heat accumulation (higher rates risk melting due to poor IR absorption). Trade-off: higher freq accelerates cleaning but requires faster scans. Recommended 30 kHz. Source: Academic literature (Appl. Surf. Sci. 2019; metal cleaning optimization).

Fluence range for removing contaminants (e.g., oxides, oils) below bronze damage threshold (~2-4 J/cm² for ablation onset); conservative 0.5-1.5 J/cm² for light cleaning, up to 3 J/cm² for heavy. Recommended 1.5 J/cm² safe for surface prep. Accounts for bronze's reflectivity (~60-70% at 1064 nm). Source: Ablation threshold studies (J. Phys. D: Appl. Phys. 2017; Cu alloys).

Beam diameter range for precise control; smaller (100-200 μm) for detailed bronze artifacts, larger (200-500 μm) for industrial surfaces. Recommended 200 μm for uniform energy distribution on bronze without pinpoint damage. Source: Equipment specs from Coherent and Trumpf laser cleaners.

Single pass for light contamination, multi-pass (2-5) for stubborn layers like patina on bronze; recommended 2 passes ensures complete removal with minimal risk to substrate integrity. Source: Industry case studies (e.g., heritage cleaning in Stud. Conserv. 2020; industrial reports from Laser Cleaning Inc.).

Ensures uniform coverage without gaps or over-ablation; 30-50% for large areas, 50-80% for fine uniformity on bronze to prevent uneven heating. Recommended 50% for balanced process efficiency. Source: Process optimization studies (Opt. Laser Technol. 2016; scanning parameter effects on metals).
